fact: October 29 is exactly 302 days into the year, which means there are only 63 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for squirrels to go absolutely nuts burying acorns - they can bury up to 10,000 acorns in a single season!
Why do leaves crunch so perfectly underfoot right now? The chlorophyll has completely broken down by late October, making leaves crispy and brittle like nature's potato chips.
October 29 falls smack in the middle of Scorpio season, and Scorpios are ruled by Pluto - the same dwarf planet that takes 248 years to orbit the sun!
The sun sets before 6 PM in most of the northern United States by October 29, but here's the weird part - it's still getting earlier by about 2 minutes each day!
Spiders are at their absolute biggest and boldest right now because they've been eating all summer long and are ready to lay eggs before winter hits.
October 29 is exactly 33 days after the autumn equinox, which means we've lost over 2 hours of daylight since summer officially ended.
In maple trees across North America, the sap has completely stopped flowing by this date, which is why the leaves turn those brilliant reds and oranges before falling.
Bats are in hyperdrive mode right now, eating up to 3,000 mosquitoes per night to fatten up before hibernation - making October 29 one of the most mosquito-free times of year!
The moon appears to rise about 50 minutes later each night, and by late October, this creates those spooky low-hanging harvest moons that look enormous on the horizon.
In the Southern Hemisphere, October 29 is like their version of late April - flowers are blooming everywhere and baby animals are being born left and right!
